profile_data.headline: Honorary Chairperson @ Asian Network for Quality | Honorary Member International Academy for Quality; CMD TQMI; Chairman ISQ
profile_data.about:
Chairman, TQM International Pvt. Ltd.       
Honorary Chairperson, Asian Network for Quality (ANQ)	        
Honorary Member of International Academy for Quality (IAQ) 
Chairman, Indian Society for Quality 	
Chairman, Intangles Lab Pvt Ltd.
Advisor, Indian Foundation for Quality Management
Mr. Mehta has the unique blend of business experience for 25 years leading to the position of Chief Executive and 35 years of consulting experience guiding organizations on variety of Total Quality Management and business excellence approaches from Europe, Japan, and the USA. He has contributed to the development of national infrastructure for quality, standardization and conformity assessment in India and has advised other countries. He contributed to global integration of quality movement as President and later Chair of International Academy for Quality (IAQ) and Chairperson of Asian Network for Quality (ANQ); He has been the founding President of Indian Society for Quality from 1996 to 2001.
For his outstanding contribution, he has been elected as Honorary Chairperson of the Asian Network for Quality (ANQ) and Honorary Member of the International Academy for Quality (IAQ) and. Mr. Mehta has chaired awards committees at IAQ, ANQ and Indian Society for Quality (ISQ) for developing and awarding many awards in India, Asia, and at the international level. Some of the Awardees are business leaders of large global corporations. 
Janak has thus unique experience of TQM & business excellence within a company, company-wide covering entire supply chain, Group wide covering all companies in a group, nation-wide, region wide and global. He covers diverse business segments from discreet manufacturing, process industry and service sector including financial, communication, IT, healthcare etc. He has helped 17 companies to challenge and win the Deming Prize, one of the most coveted enterprise level awards for Total Quality Management.
He was awarded the Deming Distinguished Service Award by Deming Prize Committee, Japan, beside many others. 
He has been a keynote speaker and invited speaker at conferences/companies around the world in 46 countries.
Mr. Mehta has been involved in setting up various institutions for quality promotion in India and overseas and continues to do so. This includes TQM Division of CII, Quality Council of India (QCI), Indian Society for Quality (ISQ), Asian Network for Quality (ANQ) and Indian Foundation for Quality Management (IFQM) as nonprofit organizations and three others for profit organizations.
Dated: December 31, 2023

multiLocaleHeadline.headline: Honorary Chairperson @ Asian Network for Quality | Honorary Member International Academy for Quality; CMD TQMI; Chairman ISQ

experience.description:
Pioneer in the promotion of quality management in India since 1982. Set up TQMI in July 1992 to offer training and consultancy in the field of quality management. It is recognized amongst the leaders in this field in India. Offerings include:
- Has been associated with 14 challenges of Deming Prize from India. Deming prize is probably the most prestigious in the world
- Six Sigma Division covering lean six sigma green belt, black belt and Design for six sigma
- Business Solutions Division covering TQM, TPM, Lean, Customer Value Management, Business Process Reengineering, New Product Development, Supplier Quality Enhancement
- Standards Product Division includes quality management, environment management, safety management etc.
- Over 27 years has worked with over 1200 organizations and trained tens of thousands people and completed thousands of projects
Guide ANQ for its policies and long term plans. Also Chairman of the ANQ Ishikawa-Kano Awards Committee
I was responsible for the management of ANQ and its operations.
Guide in development policies and long term direction. Also serve on its Awards Committee.

Elected for period 2015-2017
IAQ is "A community of the world's leading executives, practitioners and academics dedicated to promoting the cause of quality."
Purpose: "By our mutual contributions IAQ advances quality throughout the world for the benefit of humanity."
Promoted quality in over 50 countries

Elected as President on June 23, at the triennial meeting of IAQ Academicians at Budapest, Hungary for the next triennial 2012 to 2014.
Was responsible for the operation of the IAQ in fulfilling its purpose of developing new concepts and methods of quality management to contribute to the improvement of quality in various fields with a focus on education, healthcare, environment, products and services around the world.

Promotion of quality movement in the country in the wake of deregulation and economic liberalization to improve quality, productivity and customer focus.
Established TQM Division with membership of industry with 23 members that grew rapidly.
Obtained the involvement of many international organizations from the arena of quality for promotion of quality in India.  Established a close working relationship with Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers (JUSE). I took three TQM Study Missions to Japan for the CEO's of Indian companies. It provided the impetus to quality movement in India.Similar missions were taken to USA.
I was the only non Government member of the Task Force set up by the GOI to establish a system for Quality, Standardization and Conformity Assessment for India. This report resulted in the formation of Quality Council of India (QCI).

Responsible for entire operation of the company, manufacturing machine tools reporting to the Board of the company. Also had a foundry and a rolling mill
Developed systems approach to management realizing customer delight and substantial business gains.

Responsible for all technical aspects of the company including plant management and expansion including purchasing, accounts, quality and customer service.
Achieved substantial improvement in quality and productivity while reducing cost through exceptional employee involvement.

Responsible for setting up green field graphite electrode manufacturing plant. 
Achieved rated quality and productivity norms at the plant within one month of plant commissioning. It was a remarkable achievement. It was possible through the use of systems approach to management.
Responsible for brown field project for expansion of dry battery facility. Later responsible for engineering function including maintenance.
Involved in establishing a project particularly automation for dry battery facility in India. Subsequently involved in vendor development

Responsible for engineering function including maintenance and engineering stores.
Gave a talk at the IIT Kanpur on Extrusion process

post_scrape_reason: Last post date unavailable, defaulting to scrape

post_data.content:
Inspectors’ performance was measured by number of defects he captured. If defects went up he was punished. Of course, the inspector did not produce any defects as he produces nothing. So, he let defects being passed. 

Operators’ performance was measured by production he made and not the defects he produced. The customer suffered, but he had no choice. Hence no one was responsible for defects or quality. Management was happy producing selling and making money. If customer occasionally complained, we either ignored or blame the workmen. This was the scenario in India until 1970s i.e. 33 years after independence due to the controlled economic system adopted based on license raj. In addition, competition from overseas countries was discouraged through bans or high custom duties or ban on imports to protect Indian industry. It resulted in high-cost economy impacting the poorest people who bore the brunt.

I have walked into factories with ISO certifications, quality circles, and quarterly audits, still using similar practise. The quality team has grown, the documentation has multiplied, and the defect rate has not moved because the person making the part owns no part of whether the part is right.

Lucas TVS was one of the first in India, to make this change in the mid-1980s. Operators were made formally responsible for the quality of their own output, embedded in their daily measurement, their grade level, and their salary. Inspectors were redeployed into tool rooms and production support, not one person made redundant. The function of quality shifted from catching failure to preventing it. The production line was relayed as in a cell system like in Toyota Production System (TPS) with built in quality. This coincided with the establishment of Maruti Udyog Ltd. with Suzuki collaboration that established the plant using TPS in early 1980s.

The alternator line production, once this ownership was established, went from 300 units per day to 800 in eleven days, with all 800-passing quality without downstream rejection. The investment was Rs 46 lakhs, recovered in 17 months. The machines and operators had not changed. Who owned the standard had. Such transition was made in Japan in 1960s by Toyota. Many companies in India are yet to make such a change.

Watch the full podcast here: https://lnkd.in/gC8yn58S

I rarely look back at the years gone by, but your work has prompted a quiet reflection on a journey shaped by people, values, and shared purpose. I appreciate the sincerity and effort you have brought to this. My sincere thanks to you and to Caizin for making this occasion truly memorable.

author.name: Janak Mehta
author.info: Honorary Chairperson @ Asian Network for Quality | Honorary Member International Academy for Quality; CMD TQMI; Chairman ISQ

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

post_data.content:
When a defect reaches the customer, ask every function which one is responsible.

Watch what happens.

I have done this exercise in companies across India for forty years. Design points to manufacturing. Manufacturing points to procurement. Procurement points to the supplier. Sales points to the specification. Design of course can never be wrong Nobody owns the full chain. Everybody owns their section of it and nothing more.

Jairam Varadaraj named the structural areas on precisely when we spoke on our podcast.

"Organisations are managed vertically. But quality is delivered horizontally. It moves across product planning,  design, engineering, procurement manufacturing, assembly, sales, and service. The switch from function ownership to process ownership is the real transformation."

He made a point about sales that I do not hear often enough.
"How do you sell the right compressor so that the customer does not have defects?"

The quality of the sale determines the quality of the experience. A compressor specified correctly for the application will perform. The same compressor sold into the wrong application will fail and neither design nor manufacturing will appear on the warranty claim.

Every function that touches the product is part of the quality chain. In most organisations, only one or two of them believe that.

The Japanese companies I studied in the 1980s had a structural habit I noticed across all of them. Functions did not operate as separate territories with handoff points between them. The conversation was always about the process that ended with the customer receiving what they needed, and every function saw itself as responsible for that outcome, not merely for its own contribution to it.

Building that muscle is not a training programme.

It is a redesign of how accountability works and how people are reviewed against it. It takes years. Jairam described ELGi's Deming journey as the framework that gave this horizontal accountability its structure the daily management, the cross-functional management, and improvement linked to objectives and strategy. The review mechanisms that cut across the organisation chart rather than functional reviews.

When a defect reaches your customer, how many functions in your company feel genuinely responsible?

Watch the podcast here: https://lnkd.in/gtt7RrcY

TQMI ELGI EQUIPMENTS LIMITED

#TQMI #Quality #Company #Function

author.name: Janak Mehta
author.info: Honorary Chairperson @ Asian Network for Quality | Honorary Member International Academy for Quality; CMD TQMI; Chairman ISQ

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

post_data.content:
Toyota Motor Corporation has a building in Toyota city where key field failures of Toyota products since 1938 are displayed as live examples in the form of vehicles, aggregates, systems and components. There are posters and videos explaining the whole story in Japanese. Purpose is to learn from past experience and prevent similar risks in future. It is called Quality Learning Centre (QLC). All employees in Japan are encouraged to visit the QLC once a year.

History of each issue / recall was stated; what was the learning and how such information was used to improve product quality so that such issues do not repeat.

I saw it myself.

In 2023, I visited Toyota's quality learning centre as part of a delegation from the Indian Foundation for Quality Management. Mr Chandrasekhar, Chairman of the Tata Group, was part of that visit. The centre was established after the unintended acceleration crisis of 2007-8, when Toyota recalled millions of vehicles globally and faced the most serious reputational challenge in their history.

Their response was not a public relations exercise. It was a curriculum.
When we arrived and were told the session would focus on Toyota's failures rather than their achievements, there was some reluctance in our group. 

Why, when we have come all this way to learn from Japan's best, would we spend time on what went wrong?

By the end of the session, Mr Chandrasekhar said it was the most valuable part of the entire trip.

Jairam Varadaraj made a point on our podcast that I keep returning to.
"Teaching failure is more important than teaching success. Success is just the accumulated result of managed failures. Business schools build entire programmes on success cases. But in the real world, you reach success through a series of failures. How you manage those failures is the actual thing."

We do not learn to walk without falling. That lesson is obvious at one year old and forgotten by forty.

Most organisations have post-mortems. Very few make failure study a formal, recurring, protected practice. Some bury failures entirely because the discomfort of examining them is greater than the discomfort of repeating them.

The organisations that make failure a subject rather than a stigma tend to build teams that take risks more willingly, recover faster, and improve in ways that comfort-seeking organisations cannot reach.

Do not shoot the messenger who brings the bad news. I have seen many organisations where customer service function is responsible for the warranty cost. Only way service engineer can meet the objective of reducing warranty cost is by denying the warranty to the customer or the dealer.

What does your organisation do with its failures?

Watch the podcast here: https://lnkd.in/gtt7RrcY

TQMI ELGI EQUIPMENTS LIMITED

#Quality #TQMI #Organisation #Toyota

author.name: Janak Mehta
author.info: Honorary Chairperson @ Asian Network for Quality | Honorary Member International Academy for Quality; CMD TQMI; Chairman ISQ

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

post_data.content:
"Nobody is going to lose a job. Nobody's salary is going to be cut."

Jairam Varadaraj announced this to the entire company in April 2020. 

Nobody in the world knew then how long the situation would last. Some were comparing it to the Spanish flu. Three years of disruption.

I sat with this for a while after our podcast conversation.

In every difficult period I have worked through with companies over forty years, the first conversation is about cost. The first item in that conversation is almost always people. Headcount. Contracts. Salaries. Variable components that can be reduced.

Jairam had built something before the crisis arrived that made this response, in his view, not optional.

ELGi's purpose is called Conscience in Action. It has nothing to do with compressors.

He described six stakeholders who touch the company: customers, employees, suppliers, distributors, investors, and society. The purpose asks one question of every interaction with each of them. Would Jairam himself want to be treated this way if the roles were reversed?

He named a hypocrisy that most organisations carry without examining it.
"As customers, our standards are very high. As suppliers, we tend to lower them. Somewhere there is a gap between what we expect for ourselves and what we are willing to deliver to others."

Conscience in Action is the attempt to close that gap. Not as a principle in a document. As an operating instruction.

To honour the COVID commitment, the promoters were prepared to dilute their own stake to fund one and a half years of people costs. They reached the final stage of signing that agreement. Business recovered before it was needed. But the decision had already been made.

When I visited Japanese companies in 1982, what struck me was not the quality systems. It was that their purpose was for larger good. It extended beyond the product, beyond the company, toward something they called their obligation to society. I never found that language in Western companies. Only vision statements. Only mission statements.

The Indian philosophical tradition has a name for it. Yagna. Work as offering. Purpose larger than the self – benefit for all.

What Jairam has built at ELGI EQUIPMENTS LIMITED is the corporate equivalent of that.

A purpose that has nothing to do with your product turns out to have everything to do with who you are when the situation gets tough. Purpose and values are tested in difficult times and in every interaction within the company as well as outside stakeholders. When I asked a question to Prof. Kaoru Ishikawa “When an organization be considered as quality company; he responded – When the neighbours say so! I believe he meant when society says so. It was in 1986. 

Watch the podcast here: https://lnkd.in/gtt7RrcY

TQMI

author.name: Janak Mehta
author.info: Honorary Chairperson @ Asian Network for Quality | Honorary Member International Academy for Quality; CMD TQMI; Chairman ISQ

postedAt.postedAgoText: 0 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

post_data.content:
Middle management is the silent sufferer and the biggest source of inertia. I don't think we have a perfect solution yet."

Jairam Varadaraj said both of these things in the same breath on our podcast.

He runs a company ranked number 6 in the world in their category. Thirty years of transformation. And he is still working on this.

It almost never works that way.

The middle manager did not sit in the room where the strategy was built. They receive it as instruction and are expected to carry it without diluting it to people who have questions the strategy did not anticipate. They absorb every pressure from above. They carry every frustration from below. When they translate well, the organisation moves. When they do not, the gap between what was intended and what is happening widens quietly, over months, until the gap becomes the norm and nobody remembers what was originally intended.

Jairam described the role precisely.

"They take the message from the top and carry it to the bottom without diluting it. They take the message from the bottom and convert it into working solutions. They are the ones who make everything function."

He tries rotations. Cross-functional exposure. Bringing in fresh perspectives before monotony sets in. He says clearly there is no formula.

I have found this honesty more useful than most of the frameworks I have read on this subject. The practitioners who have actually led large organisations through change for long enough know that confident prescriptions about the middle layer tend to come from people who have not spent much time there.

The middle management problem is not Indian. Jairam said so. It is the same in every country, every industry, every kind of organisation.

What has genuinely worked in your organisation to keep this layer energised rather than just compliant?

Watch the podcast here: https://lnkd.in/gtt7RrcY

TQMI 
ELGI EQUIPMENTS LIMITED

author.name: Janak Mehta
author.info: Honorary Chairperson @ Asian Network for Quality | Honorary Member International Academy for Quality; CMD TQMI; Chairman ISQ

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

post_data.content:
ELGI EQUIPMENTS LIMITED walked into the US market with a lifetime, asterisk-free warranty.

No brand recognition. A Made in India label. And a promise no established competitor was willing to make.

Jairam Varadaraj told me this on our podcast and I asked what happened in the room when this decision was made.

The answer was that the decision was not made in a room. It was earned.

Before entering Europe and the US about twelve to thirteen years ago, ELGi spent years in the market doing one thing most Indian companies skip. They documented the defect levels of every major competitor. Not to benchmark themselves against those numbers. To understand the actual quality floor of the category.

Then they set their own targets exponentially beyond that floor.

The process was systematic. What was design-related. What was process-related. What was supplier-related. What came from their own sales function - selling the wrong compressor for the wrong application. All of it mapped, measured, and targeted.

Only when they reached that level did Jairam make the public commitment.
He chose the language carefully.

"Normally in any advertisement, there is a warranty and then an asterisk. At the bottom, all the disclaimers. Ours is asterisk-free. We are so confident in the quality we produce that we are offering this with no strings attached."
He was clear about what was at stake.

"If we don't have the quality and the processes to fulfil this commitment, we will be bankrupt in a second."

That sentence did as much work inside the company as the warranty did in the market.

Every function woke up to what it meant to carry that promise publicly. Design. Procurement. Manufacturing. Assembly. Sales. The warranty made the quality standard non-negotiable in a way that internal targets and review meetings had not.

Twelve years later, ELGi's failure rates and warranty costs are among the lowest in their global category. Their list prices in the US and Europe sit at par with global competitors. Not lower.

Five percent market share in Europe. Six percent in the US. For an Indian capital equipment manufacturer, selling products where a failure shuts down a factory.

The most powerful internal forcing function is sometimes a public promise that leaves no room for retreat.

TQMI
ELGI EQUIPMENTS LIMITED
